ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for coconut water as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for tropical beverages is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of coconut water is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to the liquid from one young green coconut, commonly consumed as a refreshing beverage in Southeast Asia.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut water, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170174)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw coconut water (FDC ID 170174) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values align closely with input JSON; minor rounding differences noted. Input values for potassium (250 mg), sodium (25 mg), and energy (19 kcal) are consistent with USDA data. Saturated fat estimated at 0.02 g per 100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for raw coconut water.

gi_source.title: Coconut water GI — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut water and peer-reviewed literature (Lau et al., 2014, Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists coconut water with a GI of approximately 54 (low category). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 9.84) / 100 ≈ 5.3. The input JSON cited a GI of 35 from MyHealth MOH Malaysia, which is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. The University of Sydney GI Database value of 54 is used as the approved reference. GI category remains low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (250 mg/100 g) and low sodium (25 mg/100 g) content
hypertension.guidance: Coconut water's high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation; individuals with hypertension may consume it freely as part of a DASH-aligned diet, though those on potassium-restricted regimens due to renal comorbidity should consult their clinician.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 54) and moderate natural sugar content (2.6 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 54 and approximately 7.8 g of sugar per 300 ml serving, coconut water poses a low glycemic burden and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard single-serving portions; those on strict carbohydrate-controlled plans should account for the 11.1 g total carbohydrate per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and electrolyte-rich profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Coconut water is essentially fat-free and cholesterol-free, making it a heart-friendly hydration choice; its potassium and magnesium content may support cardiac rhythm and endothelial function, and it can be recommended as a low-calorie alternative to sugar-sweetened beverages for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Magnesium content (25 mg/100 g) and anti-inflammatory hydration
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Coconut water provides magnesium and adequate hydration, both of which may support joint health; no specific contraindications exist for rheumatoid arthritis patients consuming coconut water in normal dietary quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut water (Air Kelapa in Malay; 椰子水 in Chinese; Air Kelapa Muda in Indonesian; இளநீர் (Ilanīr) in Tamil; नारियल पानी (Nariyal Pani) in Hindi) is the clear, slightly sweet liquid naturally found inside young green coconuts (Cocos nucifera). It is harvested before the coconut matures and the liquid solidifies into coconut flesh. The beverage is consumed fresh directly from the coconut shell or packaged for commercial sale across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and globally. Its flavour is mildly sweet and subtly nutty with a light, refreshing finish. The texture is thin and watery, similar to lightly flavoured water. Nutritionally, coconut water is low in calories (approximately 19 kcal per 100 ml), low in fat (0.2 g/100 g), and contains moderate natural sugars (2.6 g/100 g). It is a notable natural source of potassium (250 mg/100 g), magnesium (25 mg/100 g), calcium (24 mg/100 g), and sodium (25 mg/100 g), making it a popular natural electrolyte drink for post-exercise rehydration and hot-weather consumption. Its glycemic index is approximately 54 (low category), and a standard 300 ml serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 5.3, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation. The high potassium and low sodium profile supports blood pressure management in hypertensive individuals. Coconut water is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is widely consumed in Malaysia, Indonesia, Singapore, Thailand, and other tropical regions as a daily hydration beverage, post-workout drink, and cultural refreshment.
common_user_queries: Is coconut water good for diabetes?
How many calories in coconut water?
What is the GI of coconut water?
Is coconut water good for high blood pressure?
Can I drink coconut water every day?
How much potassium is in coconut water?
Is Air Kelapa Muda the same as coconut water?
Is coconut water good after exercise?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170174), an approved primary source with an exact match for raw coconut water. GI sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database, an approved GI-specific source. All mandatory fields are complete and internally consistent. Multilingual names for Singaporean context (Malay, Chinese, Tamil, Hindi) are now included.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw coconut water (FDC ID 170174). Input JSON values were cross-checked and found consistent with USDA data; minor rounding applied to protein (0.72 g) and carbohydrate (3.71 g) to match USDA precision. 2. Saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.00 g/100 g) are sourced from USDA SR Legacy; polyunsaturated fat is effectively trace/negligible. 3. GI value of 35 cited in the input JSON from MyHealth MOH Malaysia is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2; replaced with University of Sydney GI Database value of approximately 54 (low category). 4. Vitamin A (0.0 mcg RAE), Vitamin D (0.0 mcg), Vitamin E (0.0 mg), and Vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg) are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw coconut water. 5. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg) as coconut water is a plant-derived beverage. 6. Trans fat is confirmed zero (0.0 g) per USDA data. 7. Water content (94.99 g/100 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. 8. The food is classified as Malay/Southeast Asian cuisine. Multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are now fully addressed with the addition of Tamil (இளநீர், Ilanīr) and Hindi (नारियल पानी, Nariyal Pani) names and aliases. 9. FoodOn URI (FOODON_03301710) and FoodEx2 code (A00LC) are used as defensible ontology anchors for coconut water; SNOMED CT code is not available. 10. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as USDA SR Legacy provides sufficient coverage for all reported fields.
